elements.sent-2411-001: While Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) has shown promise in addressing distribution shifts between training and testing data, its effectiveness diminishes with heterogeneous data streams due to uniform target estimation.
elements.sent-2411-002: As previous attempts merely stabilize model fine-tuning over time to handle continually changing environments, they fundamentally assume a homogeneous target domain at any moment, leaving the intrinsic real-world data heterogeneity unresolved.
elements.sent-2411-003: This paper delves into TTA under heterogeneous data streams, moving beyond current model-centric limitations.
elements.sent-2411-004: By revisiting TTA from a data-centric perspective, we discover that decomposing samples into Fourier space facilitates an accurate data separation across different frequency levels.
elements.sent-2411-005: Drawing from this insight, we propose a novel Frequency-based Decentralized Adaptation (FreDA) framework, which transitions data from globally heterogeneous to locally homogeneous in Fourier space and employs decentralized adaptation to manage diverse distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-006: Interestingly, we devise a novel Fourier-based augmentation strategy to assist in decentralizing adaptation, which individually enhances sample quality for capturing each type of distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-007: Extensive experiments across various settings (corrupted, natural, and medical environments) demonstrate the superiority of our proposed framework over the state-of-the-arts.

elements.sent-2411-008: Deep learning models often suffer significant performance degradation when deployed in environments where the data distribution differs from that of the training set -- a challenge known as domain shift~\citep{domainshift2,domainshift .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Recently, Test-Time Adaptation (TTA)~\citep{wang2021tent,chen2022contrastive,wang2022continual,niu2022efficient,niu2022towards,su2024unraveling,press2024rdumb,deyo has emerged as a promising solution by refining model parameters to better align with the encountered data at inference time.}
elements.sent-2411-010: It leverages the incoming data stream for real-time adjustments without the need for retraining on a labeled dataset, enabling swift model adaptation to unpredictable data characteristics during deployment.
elements.sent-2411-011: Despite their success, the effectiveness of current TTA models is generally constrained within ideal testing conditions -- often involving homogeneous test samples with similar types of distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-012: While attempts have been made to address dynamic target distributions in continually changing environments~\citep{wang2022continual , they fundamentally presume a uniform target domain at any time point.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Their focus remains on enhancing model robustness against regular changes by stabilizing the fine-tuning process either by periodically resetting model weights~\citep{niu2022towards,press2024rdumb or by down-weighting samples that deviate from the estimated distribution~\citep{niu2022efficient,deyo .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Although these model-centric approaches may offer temporary relief, they do not fully recognize the intrinsic heterogeneity of real-world data.
elements.sent-2411-015: In practice, distribution shifts do not necessarily occur gradually over time but can be multifaceted at a single moment, involving heterogeneous and even conflicting shifts that current TTA models fail to adequately capture.
elements.sent-2411-016: To address this, it is crucial to understand how these heterogeneous distribution shifts impact model adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-017: When a model attempts to adjust simultaneously to multiple diverse and potentially conflicting shifts, it may encounter adaptation conflicts.
elements.sent-2411-018: Specifically, adjustments made to accommodate one type of shift can interfere with adaptations for another, as different shifts may require conflicting changes to the model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-019: For instance, adapting to variations in image brightness might necessitate parameter updates that conflict with those needed for texture changes.
elements.sent-2411-020: Such conflicts prevent models from generalizing effectively across all encountered shifts, leading to irreversible degradation in predictive capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-021: Recognizing these issues, we argue for shifting from a model-centric to a data-centric approach that proactively addresses distribution diversity in Fourier space.
elements.sent-2411-022: The rationale is that the frequency domain, unlike the common spatial domain, enables a clearer separation of data variations across different frequency levels.
elements.sent-2411-023: For example, high-frequency components are typically associated with fine-grained features like edges and textures, whereas low-frequency components generally relate to overall structural patterns such as shapes and illumination.
elements.sent-2411-024: By decomposing data into these frequency components, we can effectively isolate and manage different types of distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-025: Moreover, since the Fourier transform operates directly on the raw input images at the pixel level, it does not depend on pretrained model outputs, avoiding potential uncertainties due to significant distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-026: Importantly, this proactive separation allows us to manage distribution diversity prior to adaptation, offering a robust foundation for subsequent model enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-027: Building on this insight, we propose a Frequency-based Decentralized Adaptation (FreDA) framework.
elements.sent-2411-028: Specifically, we first dynamically partition incoming data in the Fourier domain using high-frequency information.
elements.sent-2411-029: This initial segmentation facilitates the transition from globally heterogeneous to locally homogeneous data subsets before any model adaptation occurs.
elements.sent-2411-030: On this basis, we propose a decentralized learning strategy that allows multiple local models to independently adjust to their specific data segments while periodically exchanging knowledge to form a cohesive global model.
elements.sent-2411-031: This dual approach not only captures the diversity of distribution shifts to reduce potential conflicting adaptations but also leverages periodic communication among local models to enhance the global model's generalization across multiple shifts.
elements.sent-2411-032: Furthermore, we introduce a Fourier-based augmentation mechanism paired with an entropy-based sampling strategy, which significantly increases both the quantity and quality of samples for each type of shift.
elements.sent-2411-033: This enhancement further improves the model's robustness and predictive capabilities in dynamic environments.
elements.sent-2411-034: To summarize, the main contributions of this work are three-fold:.
elements.sent-2411-035: We identify that many existing TTA methods are restricted in a model-centric paradigm that overlooks the data heterogeneity inherent in real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-036: This oversight results in ineffective adaptation when facing diverse distribution shifts simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-037: We revisit TTA from a data-centric perspective and introduce the FreDA framework.
elements.sent-2411-038: It reinterprets principles from both Fourier space and decentralized learning, leveraging specialized local adaptations to manage heterogeneous distribution shifts at test time.
elements.sent-2411-039: We conduct extensive evaluations across corrupted, natural, and medical scenarios -- demonstrating its consistent superiority under various circumstances.
elements.sent-2411-041: Test-time adaptation (TTA) aims to adjust a model \( q_{\mathbf{\theta (y|x) \), initially trained on a source dataset \( \mathcal{D _{s = \{(x,y) \sim p_s(x,y)\} \), to a target domain \( \mathcal{D _{t = \{(x,y) \sim p_t(x,y)\} \) without access to source data or target labels.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Traditionally, TTA handles covariate shift by assuming \( p_s(y|x) = p_t(y|x) \) while \( p_s(x) \neq p_t(x) \).
elements.sent-2411-043: This challenge intensifies when \( \mathcal{D _{t \) includes multiple non-i.i.d sub-distributions \( p_{t_i (x) \), complicating the adaptation process: \begin{equation}p_t(x) \rightarrow \{p_{t_1}(x), p_{t_2}(x), \dots, p_{t_N}(x)\}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Specifically, we have $\mathcal{D _t = \mathcal{D _{t_1 \cup \mathcal{D _{t_2 \cup \cdots \cup \mathcal{D _{t_N}$ where $x \in \mathcal{D _{t_1}$ satisfying $x \sim p_{t_1 (x)$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: This scenario requires the model \( q_{\mathbf{\theta (y|x) \) to effectively handle the heterogeneous and evolving target distribution to maintain robust performance.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: TTA strategies must therefore refine the model to optimize its predictive accuracy across these diverse sub-domains, ensuring consistent and reliable performance amidst significant distributional variability.. Fourier Transformation.
elements.sent-2411-047: Analyzing the frequency components of images is essential for understanding their underlying structures, and Fourier transformation plays a central role in this process.
elements.sent-2411-048: For a single-channel image \( x \), its Fourier transformation \( F(x) \) is given by: $F(x)(u, v) = \sum_{h=0 ^{H-1 \sum_{w=0 ^{W-1 x(h, w) e^{-j 2\pi \left( \frac{h {H u + \frac{w {W v \right)}$ where \( H \) and \( W \) denote the height and width of the image, respectively, and \( u \) and \( v \) are the frequency coordinates.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: The inverse Fourier transformation \( F^{-1 (x) \) allows for reconstructing the original image from its frequency spectrum, efficiently computed using the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT).}
elements.sent-2411-050: In the frequency domain, images are characterized by amplitude \( A(x) \) and phase \( P(x) \) components, derived from the real \( R(x) \) and imaginary \( I(x) \) parts of \( F(x) \): \begin{equation}A(x)(u, v) = \sqrt{R^2(x)(u, v) + I^2(x)(u, v)},\nonumber P(x)(u, v) = arctan \left( \frac{I(x)(u, v)}{R(x)(u, v)} \right), \label{fourier}\end{equation} where \( A(x) \) reveals the intensity of the frequency content, e.g., high-frequency amplitudes highlight edges and fine details while low-frequency amplitudes emphasize the overall structure and gradual changes, and \( P(x) \) encodes the position of these features within the spatial domain.

elements.sent-2411-051: This issue can be decomposed into two distinct challenges: Dependent Sampling.
elements.sent-2411-052: This problem arises when the sampling within the target stream is dependent at the class level.
elements.sent-2411-053: Existing methods~\citep{rotta,gong2022note,zhao2022delta,unmixing,roid,zhou2023ods have addressed this by aiming for class-balanced datasets during model updates, mitigating risks associated with class imbalance over time.}
elements.sent-2411-054: They typically adjust sample proportions based on pseudo labels or extend data collection periods to reduce dependent sampling.
elements.sent-2411-055: However, unlike these methods that concentrate on mitigating class-level imbalances, our work focuses on enhancing TTA models in the presence of diverse sample styles or mixed distributions.
elements.sent-2411-056: We address data heterogeneity at the sample level, aiming to improve model adaptation capabilities in face of varying distribution shifts that are not captured by class balancing techniques.
elements.sent-2411-057: Notably, although our method is not tailored for class-dependent issues, our experimental results demonstrate that when class-dependent and mixed distributions coexist, our approach still achieves the best performance -- showcasing the broad applicability of our model design.. Mixed Distributions.

elements.sent-2411-060: These model-centric approaches fail to capture the actual data heterogeneity encountered in practice, causing model degradation in real-world deployment.
elements.sent-2411-061: In contrast, our work re-examines \textcolor{black {this problem from a data-centric perspective.}}

elements.sent-2411-063: Although a recent work~\citep{niu2022towards also consider mixed distribution scenarios, their study targets a broader ``Dynamic Wild World'' topic without delving deeply into this data heterogeneity problem.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Conversely, our study focuses on investigating and managing heterogeneous data streams in TTA and proposing tailored strategies to address this problem.
elements.sent-2411-065: Test-time Adaptation under mixed distribution resembles the multi-target unsupervised domain adaptation (MT-UDA) setting~\citep{mt1,mt2,ocda1,ocda2 , where multiple domains exist within the target domain.}
elements.sent-2411-066: However, TTA introduces complexities that far exceed those in conventional MT-UDA settings, primarily due to: 1) Inaccessible Labeled Source Data -- In TTA, the labeled source distribution is not available, making it challenging to leverage source-target dissimilarities directly.

elements.subsection-2411-003: Decentralized learning, federated learning, and distributed learning
elements.sent-2411-069: This work also intersects with decentralized, federated, and distributed learning due to our approach of splitting data batches into disjoint subsets and applying decentralized model adaptation: 1) Decentralized learning typically focuses on learning from decentralized, non-i.i.d.. data~\citep{hsieh2020non .}
elements.sent-2411-070: In this work, however, the data is not originally decentralized; all target samples arrive together, while we proactively split them into disjoint subsets, revealing latent non-i.i.d.
elements.sent-2411-071: In our case, as target samples are mixed in a batch, data privacy is not a constraint.
elements.sent-2411-072: However, like federated learning, our approach also involves model collaboration where multiple local models periodically share insights to form a cohesive global model.
elements.sent-2411-073: In contrast, our method operates in a real-time fine-tuning context with limited data at one time, hence scalability is less of a concern.
elements.sent-2411-074: In recent years, frequency-based techniques have become integral to transfer learning strategies.
elements.sent-2411-075: For domain generalization, extensive studies apply frequency analysis to gain insights into the learning behavior and model robustness of DNNs~\citep{freq1,freq2,freq3,freq4 .}
elements.sent-2411-076: Some approaches leverage data augmentation to improve model performance.
elements.sent-2411-077: They implement adversarial training to identify the most impactful augmentations~\citep{freq6,freq7 or introducing generated Fourier-basis functions as additive noise~\citep{freq5,vaish2024fourier complementary to visual augmentations.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: In domain adaptation, researchers implement data augmentation processes involving linear interpolation between the amplitude spectra of images with different styles ~\citep{fda1,fda2,fda3,fda4 .}
elements.sent-2411-079: This methodology effectively reduces domain discrepancies and mitigates the risk of over-fitting to low-level statistical details inherent in amplitude information.
elements.sent-2411-080: Motivated by these advancements, we observe that frequency information is a potent domain characteristic.
elements.sent-2411-081: This realization, combined with the insight that augmentations to the amplitude component can mitigate overfitting, particularly due to the uniform amplitude distributions resulting from prior clustering, forms the core inspiration for our FreDA approach.
elements.sent-2411-082: While our proposed frequency-domain perturbation strategy shares the underlying idea of manipulating the amplitude component with these prior works, we distinguish ourselves by introducing subtle perturbations directly to the original amplitude components of carefully selected samples.
elements.sent-2411-083: This strategy is better suited to unsupervised fine-tuning task, where the TTA process is sensitive, and excessive augmentation may cause model degradation.
elements.section-2411-004: TTA under Mixed Distribution Shifts: A Fourier Perspective
elements.sent-2411-084: Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) methods have been instrumental in managing domain shifts under a single type of target distribution.
elements.sent-2411-085: However, their effectiveness significantly diminishes under scenarios involving multiple distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-086: This is evident as models exhibit a marked decrease in sample class discriminability on the same dataset when exposed to mixed target distributions, as demonstrated by comparing Figure~\ref{fig:test (a) and (b).}
elements.sent-2411-087: A direct approach to addressing this issue involves segregating samples belonging to different target distributions.
elements.sent-2411-088: However, in real-time applications, the specific target subdomains from which incoming samples originate, or whether they conform to the same distribution shifts, are generally agnostic.
elements.sent-2411-089: Attempting to cluster samples based on features extracted by the model can be misleading, as samples from different distributions may exhibit similarities due to belonging to the same category, resulting in poor separability of different target subdomains as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:test (c).}
elements.sent-2411-090: Interestingly, when clustering is based directly on the high-frequency information of the samples -- without relying on model-derived feature extractors -- a significant distinction can be made between samples from different target distributions, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:test (d).}
elements.sent-2411-091: This observation is not unexpected, considering high-frequency information typically captures variations in image textures and styles, focusing more on the underlying differences in data distributions.
elements.sent-2411-092: Building on the experimental observations and analysis outlined above, the following section proposes leveraging the frequency domain for enhancing the adaptability of TTA methods in more realistic settings involving mixed distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-093: The previous discussions highlight how heterogeneity within target distribution can hinder model adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-094: This raises a natural question: How can we manage this distributional heterogeneity to achieve better adaptation? As established in our earlier section, effectively distinguishing samples associated with different distribution shifts is vital for successful domain adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-095: Moreover, the similarity in high-frequency information of samples provides a strong indication of whether they belong to the same or different target distributions.
elements.sent-2411-096: Building upon these findings, we tackle the TTA problem by capitalizing on the high-frequency data components and propose a novel Frequency-based Decentralized Adaptation (FreDA) framework.
elements.sent-2411-097: It employs a data-centric approach to partition target samples into multiple homogeneous subdomains in Fourier space, enabling an accurate model adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-098: This strategy is complemented by a novel frequency-based augmentation technique that enriches each target subdomain with augmented samples, thereby further bolstering model adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-099: The overall pipeline of FreDA is detailed in Aglorithm~\ref{algo:FreDA .}
elements.sent-2411-100: Insight: Fourier transform offers an effective method to extract different frequency components from images, with high-frequency information particularly useful for capturing fine-grained details such as texture and noise.
elements.sent-2411-101: These details often highlight subtle variations among different distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-102: By harnessing high-frequency components from images, we can distinguish samples that lead to different distribution shifts within a TTA setting through a simple clustering technique.
elements.sent-2411-103: Solution: Based on this intuitive insight, we propose a new module called Frequency-based Decentralized Learning.
elements.sent-2411-104: This module leverages frequency information directly extracted from the pixel space to systematically partition data into multiple homogeneous subsets, enabling multiple local models to specialize in capturing each distribution shift individually (see Figure~\ref{fig:benner ).}
elements.sent-2411-105: Concurrently, our method enhances collaborative learning by allowing periodic parameter sharing among these local models, thereby boosting the overall model adaptability to diverse distribution shifts.. Frequency Feature Extraction.
elements.sent-2411-106: We start by extracting frequency domain features from the input images to identify distinct distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-107: Let $\mathbf{X \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times c \times h \times w}$ denote a batch of input images, where $n$ is the batch size, $c$ is the number of channels, $h$ and $w$ are the height and width of the images.}}
elements.sent-2411-108: We first apply a Fourier transform $\mathcal{F}$ to each image $\mathbf{X _i$ to obtain its frequency domain representation $\mathcal{F (\mathbf{X _i) \in \mathbb{C ^{h \times w \times c}$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-110: We then employ a clustering algorithm (e.g., K-means) to partition the frequency features into $K$ clusters, each corresponding to a different type of distribution shift.

Parameter Aggregation.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-113: To integrate knowledge from all subnetworks and prevent degradation on specific subdomains, we perform an aggregation of their parameters: \begin{equation}\mathbf{\theta}_{\text{global}} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \left( \frac{|\mathcal{D}_k|}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} |\mathcal{D}_j|} \mathbf{\theta}_k \right), \label{comm}\end{equation} where \( |\mathcal{D _k| \) denotes the number of samples in cluster \( k \).}
elements.sent-2411-114: This aggregation step combines the parameter updates from each subnetwork proportionally to its cluster size.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Framework of Frequency-based Decentralized Learning and Augmentation}\label{algo:FreDA} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require Step $t$, Input $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times h \times w \times c}$, Pretrained source model $q_\theta$, Initialize Feature Repository $\mathcal{R} \leftarrow \emptyset$, CLUSTER\_NUM $K$, KMEANS\_SIZE $N$, COMM\_INTERVAL $f$; \Statex \textbf{\textcolor{black!40}{Step 1: Extract Frequency Features}} \For{$i = 1$ to $n$} \State $\mathbf{A}_{hf,i} \leftarrow \text{vec}(\text{G}(\mathbf{X}_i))$ \Comment{\textit{Extract high-freq components (Eq.~\ref{fourier},~\ref{hf1})}} \EndFor \Statex \textbf{\textcolor{black!40}{Step 2: Dynamic Clustering}} \State $\mathcal{R} \leftarrow \mathcal{R} \cup \{\mathbf{A}_{hf,i}\}_{i=1}^n$ \Comment{Frequency Information Repository} \State $\mathcal{R} \leftarrow \mathcal{R}[(|\mathcal{R}| - N + 1) : ]$ \Comment{Keep the last $N$ entries for kmeans clustering} \State $(\mathbf{C}_t, \mathbf{Z}) \leftarrow \text{K-means}(\mathcal{R}, K, \mathbf{C}_{t-1})$ \Comment{\textit{Obtain Cluster Labels $\mathbf{Z} = \{Z_i\}_{i=1}^{n}$ (Eq.~\ref{kmeans})}} \Statex \textbf{\textcolor{black!40}{Step 3: Local Model Training}} \For {cluster $k \in \{1, \ldots, K\}$} \State $\mathcal{S}_k \leftarrow \mathcal{S}_k \cup \{\mathbf{X}_i \mid Z_i = k\}$ \Comment{Gather samples for cluster $k$} \State $\mathcal{S}_k \leftarrow \mathcal{S}_k[(|\mathcal{S}_k| - n + 1) : ]$ \Comment{Keep the last $batch\_size = n$ entries} \State $\mathcal{S}_k' \leftarrow \text{select\_samples}(\mathcal{S}_k)$ \Comment{\textit{Select samples (Eq.~\ref{select})}} \For{each $\mathbf{X}_i \in \mathcal{S}_k'$} \State $\tilde{\mathbf{X}}_i \leftarrow \text{augment}(\mathbf{X}_i)$ \Comment{\textit{Augment data (Eq.~\ref{generation})}} \State $\text{Train}(q_{\theta_k},\mathbf{X}_i, \tilde{\mathbf{X}}_i)$ \Comment{\textit{Train local model (Eq.~\ref{update})}} \EndFor \EndFor \Statex \textbf{\textcolor{black!40}{Step 4: Compile Predictions}} \State $\mathbf{Y} \leftarrow \text{collect\_sort}(\{q_{\theta_k}(\mathbf{X})\})$ \Comment{Collect and sort predictions} \Statex \textbf{\textcolor{black!40}{Step 5: Global Model Communication}} \State If t \% $f$ == 0 : \Comment{\textit{Model Communication with interval $f$ (Eq.\ref{comm}, \ref{distribute})}} \State \quad $\mathbf{\theta}_{\text{global}} \leftarrow \sum_{k=1}^K w_k \theta_k$ \State \quad$\mathbf{\theta}_k \leftarrow \mathbf{\theta}_{\text{global}}$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-127: To generate a perturbed amplitude spectrum $\tilde{A (\mathbf{F _i)$, we apply a random Gaussian perturbation: \begin{equation}\tilde{A}(\mathbf{F}_i) = \left( 1 + \alpha \cdot \Delta \right) \cdot A(\mathbf{F}_i), \label{permute}\end{equation} where $\Delta \sim \mathcal{N (0, \sigma^2)$ is a perturbation matrix sampled from a Gaussian distribution, and $\alpha$ is a scaling factor.}}}
elements.sent-2411-128: Then, the augmented sample $\tilde{\mathbf{X _i$ is reconstructed via the inverse Fourier transform to the perturbed amplitude spectrum, combined with the original phase spectrum $P(\mathbf{F _i)$: \begin{equation}\tilde{\mathbf{X}}_i = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\left(\tilde{A}(\mathbf{F}_i), P(\mathbf{F}_i)\right). \label{generation}\end{equation} Loss Function.}}}
elements.sent-2411-129: The training objective combines the entropy loss of the selected samples with a consistency loss from the augmented samples.
elements.sent-2411-130: The total loss is defined as: \begin{equation}\label{lossall} \mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} H(\mathbf{y}_i) + \lambda \cdot \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathcal{L}_{\text{con}}\left(\hat{\mathbf{y}}_i, \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_i\right),\end{equation} where the entropy loss $H(\mathbf{y _i)$ for the original samples is given by $H(\mathbf{y _i) = - \sum_{j=1 ^{C \mathbf{y _{i,j \log \mathbf{y _{i,j}$ with $\mathbf{y _i$ being the predicted probability over the $C$ classes for the sample $\mathbf{X _i$, and the consistency loss $\mathcal{L _{\text{con }\left(\tilde{\mathbf{y _i, \hat{\mathbf{y _i\right)$ is defined as the cross-entropy between the prediction $\tilde{\mathbf{y _i$ of the augmented sample $\tilde{\mathbf{X _i$ and the pseudo-label $\hat{\mathbf{y _i$ from the original sample: $\mathcal{L _{\text{con }\left(\hat{\mathbf{y _i, \tilde{\mathbf{y _i\right) = - \sum_{j=1 ^{C \hat{\mathbf{y _{i,j \log \tilde{\mathbf{y _{i,j}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-132: To provide a comprehensive evaluation of TTA deployment, we test models over multiple datasets under three different scenarios:.

elements.sent-2411-136: Natural Domain Shifts: We extend our evaluation to DomainNet126~\citep{domainnet126 , which presents natural shifts across four domains (Real, Clipart, Painting, Sketch) encompassing 126 classes, representing a subset of the larger DomainNet dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-137: Medical Application: Models are further evaluated on Camelyon17~\citep{bandi2018detection , comprising over 450,000 histopathological patches from lymph node sections for binary classification of normal and tumor tissue, with data originating from five distinct healthcare centers.}
elements.sent-2411-138: For corruption datasets, the model is pretrained on the clean dataset and the 15 corruptions are randomly mixed as the target distribution.
elements.sent-2411-139: We leverage the highest severity = 5 in all the experiments.

elements.sent-2411-150: FreDA consistently improves across different distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-151: Our method consistently attains the lowest classification error rates across all evaluated datasets (see Table~\ref{tab:performance_evaluation and~\ref{tab:performance_evaluation2 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-152: Notably, on the Camelyon17 dataset, FreDA reduced the error rate to 27.9\%, outperforming the next best method by 5.9\%.
elements.sent-2411-153: This significant improvement is particularly notable where other approaches falter if compared with no training (TBN), meaning they struggle to adapt to the complex medical imaging data.
elements.sent-2411-154: By effectively handling high variability and intricate patterns in the data, FreDA maintains superior accuracy and adaptability.
elements.sent-2411-155: FreDA effectively mitigates both covariate and label shifts.
elements.sent-2411-156: In environments characterized by simultaneous covariate and label shifts, our approach keep showing exceptional adaptability (see Table~\ref{tab:noniidboth ).}
elements.sent-2411-157: We attribute this success to FreDA's ability to separate covariate shifts from label shifts via decentralized learning.
elements.sent-2411-158: FreDA achieves this by first isolating target different distribution shifts and then focus on learning label shifts for each specific distribution.
elements.sent-2411-159: This sequential approach prevents models from being overwhelmed by simultaneous shifts, allowing it to address each type of shift independently and effectively.

elements.sent-2411-200: Our results suggest that the proposed FreDA not only excels in its intended mixed domain scenarios but also generalizes effectively to standard continual adaptation tasks, providing a robust solution across various distributional challenges.
elements.sent-2411-201: In this section, we study the parameter choice of CLUSTER\_NUM, KMEANS\_SIZE and COMM\_INTERVAL (refer to Algorithm~\ref{algo:FreDA for detailed definitions).}
elements.sent-2411-203: As we adjust the KMEANS\_SIZE parameter from 256 to 2048, there is a remarkably consistent performance on different datasets, indicating that our method's capability to generalize across various sizes.
elements.sent-2411-204: The variation in CLUSTER\_NUM across our datasets underscores the nuanced balance required in selecting the optimal branch number for domain adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-205: Utilizing just two clusters already yields relatively good results, suggesting that a minimal decentralization can be effective.
elements.sent-2411-206: However, as the number of clusters increases from 2 to 16, we observe a decline in performance on CIFAR100-C and a more pronounced deterioration on ImageNet-C, with the optimal performance achieved at a CLUSTER\_NUM of 4.
elements.sent-2411-207: This trend underscores the delicate trade-off between model complexity and the risk of overfitting: employing too large a cluster size can lead to a model overly tailored to the training data, impairing its generalization capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-208: For the sensitivity analysis of COMM\_INTERVAL, we observe that our method is generally robust to changes in the communication interval across all datasets.
elements.sent-2411-209: However, the impact of communication frequency varies significantly among different datasets.
elements.sent-2411-210: For simpler datasets like CIFAR10, minimal communication, exemplified by an interval of $f=1000$, yields the best results.
elements.sent-2411-211: This could be attributed to the model's high accuracy, enabling positive feedback loops even within isolated branches.
elements.sent-2411-212: Conversely, for more complex datasets, more frequent communication, with intervals as low as $f=1$, appears beneficial.
elements.sent-2411-213: This frequent updating may help prevent model degradation over time, especially in scenarios where the data complexity could lead to significant divergences in learning pathways among distributed model components.

elements.sent-2411-225: Deploying deep models in real-world scenarios remains challenging due to significant performance drops under distribution shifts between training and deployment environments.
elements.sent-2411-226: Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) has recently emerged as a promising solution, enabling on-the-fly model adaptation without access to source data.
elements.sent-2411-227: However, its effectiveness degrades significantly in the presence of complex, mixed distribution shifts -- common in practical settings -- where multiple latent domains coexist.
elements.sent-2411-228: Adapting under such intrinsic heterogeneity, especially in unlabeled and online conditions, remains an open and underexplored challenge.
elements.sent-2411-229: In this paper, we study TTA under mixed distribution shifts and move beyond conventional \red{homogeneous adaptation paradigms.}
elements.sent-2411-230: By \red{revisiting TTA from a frequency-domain perspective, we observe that distribution heterogeneity often manifests in Fourier space -- for instance, high-frequency components tend to carry domain-specific variations.}
elements.sent-2411-231: This motivates us to perform domain-aware separation using high-frequency texture cues, making diverse shift patterns more tractable.
elements.sent-2411-232: To this end, we propose FreDA, a novel Frequency-based Decentralized Adaptation framework that decomposes globally heterogeneous data into locally homogeneous components in the frequency domain.
elements.sent-2411-233: It further employs decentralized learning and augmentation strategies to robustly adapt under complex, evolving shifts.
elements.sent-2411-234: Extensive experiments across various environments (corrupted, natural, and medical) demonstrate the superiority of our proposed framework over the state-of-the-arts.
elements.sent-2411-235: Recently, Test-Time Adaptation (TTA)~\cite{wang2021tent,chen2022contrastive,wang2022continual,niu2022efficient,niu2022towards,su2024unraveling,press2024rdumb,deyo has emerged as a promising solution by refining model parameters to better align with the encountered data at inference time.}
elements.sent-2411-236: Despite their success, current TTA models often are limited to ideal testing conditions, typically involving homogeneous test samples with similar types of distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-237: In reality, distribution shifts are mixed, overlapping, and even conflicting~\cite{mt1,mt2,yao2025scmix,imagenetc,su2024navigating .}
elements.sent-2411-238: For instance, {photo management software handles diverse corruptions, such as noise, blur, compression; medical imaging platforms manage inconsistencies from varied acquisition methods; and autonomous driving systems face fluctuating conditions like lighting, weather, and road types.}
elements.sent-2411-239: Such complexity poses serious challenges for existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-240: While recent efforts have extended TTA to continually changing environments~\cite{wang2022continual , they typically assume a uniform target domain at each time step, as illustrated in the continual setting in Figure~\ref{fig:tta_problem ~(a).}}
elements.sent-2411-241: Some methods periodically reset the model to its source pre-trained state~\cite{niu2022towards,press2024rdumb to mitigate the cumulative effect of sequential distribution shifts on adaptation.}
elements.sent-2411-242: Others down-weight outlier target samples~\cite{niu2022efficient,deyo to suppress the impact of abrupt or rare shifts.}
elements.sent-2411-243: Although these strategies aim to improve robustness under dynamic conditions, they fall short when facing the entangled / mixed real-world distributions (see Figure~\ref{fig:tta_problem ~(b)).}
elements.sent-2411-244: Their shortcomings stem from a shared limitation: they lack the capacity to disentangle or localize domain variations.
elements.sent-2411-245: As a result, they often overfit to dominant patterns or suffer from \red{catastrophic forgetting , failing to be deployed in realistic, heterogeneous data streams.}
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{tta_problem.pdf} \caption{(a) Illustration of three TTA scenarios with single, continual, and mixed domain shifts. (b) Classification error rate on CIFAR-10-C across different TTA settings: performance of conventional models drops sharply under mixed domain shifts. } \label{fig:tta_problem}
elements.sent-2411-246: Capturing such intrinsic heterogeneity under unlabeled, online conditions is particularly challenging, and effective adaptation in this setting remains an open problem.
elements.sent-2411-247: To tackle this, we propose shifting from coarse, \red{homogeneous adaptation to a fine-grained strategy that explicitly disentangles heterogeneous shifts.}
elements.sent-2411-248: Our approach leverages the frequency information to disentangle domain variations, which naturally separates data features across frequency bands: high-frequency components capture fine details like edges and textures, while low-frequency components represent global structures such as shape and illumination (Section~\ref{sec:high_freq_effect ).}
elements.sent-2411-249: By decomposing inputs accordingly, we better characterize distributional variations and identify diverse shifts.
elements.sent-2411-250: Moreover, the Fourier transform operates directly on raw pixel-level inputs, which shields the method from performance degradation under large domain gaps, and also enhancing adaptability and deployment efficiency in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-251: Building upon this insight, we introduce a framework, termed Frequency-based Decentralized Adaptation (FreDA).
elements.sent-2411-252: FreDA begins by partitioning incoming data in the Fourier domain, where high-frequency components are used to group samples with similar shift characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-253: This transforms globally heterogeneous inputs into locally homogeneous subsets before any adaptation takes place.
elements.sent-2411-254: Based on these partitions, FreDA deploys multiple local models, each assigned to a specific subset.
elements.sent-2411-255: These models adapt independently to their respective data streams while periodically synchronizing through parameter exchange.
elements.sent-2411-256: The aggregated parameters form a shared base model, which is then used to reinitialize the local models, ensuring continual and coordinated adaptation across domains.
elements.sent-2411-257: This approach not only alleviates clustering errors via collaborative knowledge sharing but also allows local models to capture diverse distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-258: To further enhance robustness, we introduce a novel Fourier-based augmentation scheme that improves sample quality and strengthens adaptation to shift-specific characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-259: We identify a key limitation of most existing TTA methods -- their neglect of real-world data heterogeneity -- which leads to suboptimal performance when confronted with mixed and diverse distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-260: We propose FreDA, a frequency-based decentralized adaptation framework that leverages spectral decomposition and localized adaptation to effectively address heterogeneous distribution shifts at test-time.
elements.sent-2411-261: We validate FreDA through extensive experiments on corrupted, natural, and medical benchmarks, demonstrating consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods across various TTA scenarios.
elements.subsection-2411-007: Transfer Learning, Domain Adaptation, Test-time Training, and Test-time Adaptation
elements.sent-2411-262: Deep neural networks often experience performance degradation when deployed in environments different from their training settings, due to distribution shifts between source (training) and target (testing) domains.
elements.sent-2411-263: This issue is extensively studied under the umbrella of transfer learning~\cite{pan2009survey,zhang2022transfer , which aims to transfer knowledge across domains.}
elements.sent-2411-264: A key branch of transfer learning is domain adaptation (DA)~\cite{DA1,DA2,DA3,DA4 , where models trained on a labeled source domain are adapted to a target domain.}
elements.sent-2411-265: Depending on the availability of source and target data, DA can be classified into: Supervised DA using labeled data in both source and target domains during training; Unsupervised DA, which relies on labeled source data and unlabeled target data; and Source-free DA, that adapts to the target domain without access to source data, typically due to privacy or transmission constraints.
elements.figure-2411-014: \caption{Comparison of different transfer learning settings.} \label{tab:da_comparison} \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.4} \setlength\tabcolsep{2pt} 0.48\textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{lcccc} \multirow{2}{*}{Topic} \multirow{2}{*}{\shortstack{Source Data}} \multirow{2}{*}{\shortstack{Target Labels}} \multirow{2}{*}{\shortstack{Online Adaptation}} \multirow{2}{*}{\shortstack{Model Agnostic}} {Supervised Domain Adaptation} $\checkmark$ $\checkmark$ $\times$ -- {Unsupervised Domain Adaptation} $\checkmark$ $\times$ $\times$ -- {Source-free Domain Adaptation} $\times$ $\times$ $\times$ -- {Test-time Training} $\times$ $\times$ $\checkmark$ $\times$ \rowcolor{gray!10} {Test-time Adaptation} $\times$ $\times$ $\checkmark$ $\checkmark$ \end{tabular} } \caption*{ Note: TTA is the most practical setting for real-world deep model deployment.}
elements.sent-2411-266: To address this, two prominent test-time adaptation paradigms have been proposed: Test-time Training (TTT)~\cite{TTT,liu2021ttt++,TTT_tkde introduces an auxiliary task -- often self-supervised -- during pre-training.}
elements.sent-2411-267: At test time, the model optimizes this auxiliary objective to adapt to the target distribution accordingly; \red{Test-time Adaptation (TTA)~\cite{wang2021tent,niu2022towards,su2024unraveling poses a more demanding and practical scenario, where the model must adapt on-the-fly to the test stream without any prior modification during training.}}
elements.sent-2411-268: This setting emphasizes the need for rapid, real-time model updates to effectively capture the continuously incoming data.
elements.sent-2411-269: A brief comparison of these adaptation settings is summarized in TABLE~\ref{tab:da_comparison .}
elements.sent-2411-270: Our work falls under the TTA setting, where we propose a generalizable and robust adaptation framework that maintains strong performance across diverse distribution shifts and corruptions -- without relying on source data or altering the pre-training process.
elements.sent-2411-271: While conventional TTA methods primarily focus on single domain shifts under the idealized independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) assumption, real-world deployment often violates these conditions due to inherent data heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-272: This discrepancy has motivated recent efforts to extend TTA to more realistic settings, with two primary challenges:.
elements.sent-2411-273: Modern TTA methods designed for continual domain shifts~\cite{wang2022continual,rotta,niu2022efficient,press2024rdumb,deyo have pushed beyond the single adaptation setting.}
elements.sent-2411-274: However, these approaches assume that the target domain is uniform at each time point -- a special case of mixed domains where the change occurs across distinct time periods.
elements.sent-2411-275: In reality, data streams often involve genuinely mixed domains with heterogeneous distributions present simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-276: Existing approaches primarily focus on stabilizing model updates through periodic parameter resets~\cite{niu2022towards,press2024rdumb or importance weighting of target samples~\cite{niu2022efficient,press2024rdumb,deyo , but these techniques are ill-equipped to disentangle and manage the complex interleaving of domain shifts, leading to degraded performance.}}
elements.sent-2411-277: In contrast, our work adopts a data-centric perspective by explicitly addressing mixed domains via frequency-space decomposition, which facilitates domain separation and proactive distribution alignment prior to model adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-278: While recent efforts like~\cite{niu2022towards acknowledge mixed distribution challenges under the broader ``Dynamic Wild World'' paradigm, their unified treatment of diverse real-world factors does not systematically address data heterogeneity.}
elements.sent-2411-279: Conversely, our study directly targets the core issue of mixed domains in TTA, introducing tailored strategies for disentangling and adapting to co-occurring heterogeneous distributions.
elements.figure-2411-015: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth,clip, trim=14.4cm 4.5cm 18cm 2cm]{high_freq_sep.pdf} \caption{ (a) Visual heterogeneity across five healthcare centers in Camelyon17 dataset: Example patches showcase diverse domain shifts that characterize mixed target domains. (b) Conventional sample latent features from pretrained models fail to separate different target subdomains, showing significant overlap. (c) High-frequency information enable distinct separation of target subdomains, contrasting with the conventional ones. } \label{fig:high_freq_effect}
elements.sent-2411-280: The second challenge stems from class-level dependencies introduced by temporal data sampling.
elements.sent-2411-281: This topic has garnered significant research attention, with a growing body of work~\cite{rotta,gong2022note,zhao2022delta,unmixing,roid,zhou2023ods actively addressing it through strategies such as pseudo-label-based rebalancing, or extended observation windows.}
elements.sent-2411-282: These approaches aim to correct the temporal imbalance caused by skewed class distributions over time.
elements.sent-2411-283: However, unlike this these studies that focus on class-level imbalance, our work diverges by targeting sample-level heterogeneity, which remains underexplored.
elements.sent-2411-284: Specifically, we address style diversity and distributional variation at test-time -- a challenge that persist even under class-balanced conditions -- to improve model robustness under mixed-domain scenarios.
elements.section-2411-014: Problem Definition of mixed domain shifts
elements.sent-2411-285: Test-time adaptation (TTA) aims to adjust a model \( q_{\mathbf{\theta (y|x) \), initially trained on a source dataset \( \mathcal{D _{s = \{(x,y) \sim p_s(x,y)\} \), to a target domain \( \mathcal{D _{t = \{(x,y) \sim p_t(x,y)\} \) on a data stream without accessing source data or target labels.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-286: TTA handles covariate shift by assuming \( p_s(y|x) = p_t(y|x) \) while \( p_s(x) \neq p_t(x) \).
elements.sent-2411-287: This challenge intensifies when \( \mathcal{D _{t \) contains multiple non-i.i.d sub-distributions \( p_{t_i (x) \), such as \begin{equation}p_t(x) \gets \{p_{t_1}(x), p_{t_2}(x), \dots, p_{t_N}(x)\}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-288: TTA strategies must therefore refine the model to optimize its predictive accuracy across these diverse sub-domains, ensuring consistent and reliable performance amidst significant distributional variability.

elements.sent-2411-289: While Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) methods excel under single-type distribution shifts, their performance degrades catastrophically when facing mixed distribution shifts -- a ubiquitous challenge in real-world deployment.
elements.sent-2411-290: As demonstrated on CIFAR-10-C (Figure~\ref{fig:tta_problem ~(b)), leading methods like TENT~\cite{wang2021tent , SAR~\cite{niu2022towards and RoTTA~\cite{rotta suffer \red{16\% average accuracy drop compared to single-shift scenarios.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-291: Conventional TTA paradigms attempt to mitigate this through passive robustness measures, such as entropy regularization~\cite{wang2021tent , gradient masking~\cite{niu2022towards , or model resetting~\cite{press2024rdumb .}}}
elements.sent-2411-292: These methods treat mixed distribution shifts as irreducible noise, deferring corrective actions until model failures occur -- a reactive paradigm that inevitably compromises adaptation efficacy.
elements.sent-2411-293: To address this, we propose a paradigm shift to proactive distribution management -- instead of demanding models to handle arbitrary mixtures, we first resolve the heterogeneity via frequency-space decomposition.
elements.sent-2411-294: Our key insight is that mixed distributions exhibit spectral signatures that can be disentangled before model adaptation begins.
elements.sent-2411-295: As evidenced in Figure~\ref{fig:high_freq_effect ~(c), high-frequency components naturally separate distinct shifts without relying on error-prone model features (see Figure~\ref{fig:high_freq_effect ~(b).}}
elements.sent-2411-296: This theoretically grounded decomposition converts the ill-posed mixed-shift adaptation into multiple well-conditioned homogeneous-shift problems, effectively create an ideal adaptation environment from chaotic real-world streams.
elements.sent-2411-297: Crucially, this decomposition enables a decentralized adaptation where we can deploy domain-specific models tailored to high-frequency-separated clusters.
elements.sent-2411-298: Take TBN~\cite{nado2020evaluating as an example: while its centralized variant recalculates batch normalization (BN) statistics globally (treating heterogeneous data as a single distribution), our decentralized adaptation computes localized BN parameters within each spectrally disentangled cluster.}
elements.sent-2411-299: As shown in Figu~\ref{fig:comparedClassVisual , this context-aware estimation preserves semantic discriminability: samples exhibit tighter within-class cohesion and sharper between-class boundaries compared to the blurred separability of centralized adaptation.}
elements.sent-2411-300: Building on these insights, the following section details how leveraging the frequency domain enhances TTA methods for realistic scenarios with mixed distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-301: The previous discussion highlights how heterogeneity within target distributions can hinder effective model adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-302: This naturally raises the question: How can we manage such distributional heterogeneity to enable better adaptation? As outlined earlier, distinguishing samples associated with different distribution shifts offers a promising pathway.
elements.sent-2411-303: Building on this insight, we address the TTA problem by leveraging high-frequency components in the data and propose \red{a novel framework termed Frequency-based Decentralized Adaptation (FreDA) .}
elements.sent-2411-304: It partitions target samples into multiple homogeneous subdomains in the Fourier space, facilitating more accurate model adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-305: This is further enhanced by a frequency-based augmentation strategy that enriches each subdomain with diverse samples, strengthening model robustness.
elements.sent-2411-306: Fourier transform offers an effective method to extract different frequency components from images, with high-frequency information particularly useful for capturing fine-grained details such as texture and noise.
elements.sent-2411-307: Based on this insight, we propose a module termed Frequency-based Decentralized Learning.
elements.sent-2411-308: It leverages frequency information extracted from the pixel space to systematically partition data into multiple homogeneous subsets, enabling multiple local models to specialize in capturing each distribution shift individually.
elements.sent-2411-309: Concurrently, our method enables collaborative learning by allowing periodic parameter sharing among these local models via an enhanced base model, thereby boosting overall adaptability to diverse distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-310: We start by extracting frequency domain features from the input images.
elements.sent-2411-311: Let $\mathbf{X \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times c \times h \times w}$ denote a batch of input images, where $n$ is the batch size, $c$ is the number of channels, $h$ and $w$ are the height and width.}}
elements.sent-2411-312: We first apply a Fourier transform $\mathcal{F}$ to each image $\mathbf{x _i$.}
elements.sent-2411-313: This transform converts the image from the spatial domain to the frequency domain, producing a complex-valued representation \(\mathcal{F (\mathbf{x _i) \in \mathbb{C ^{h \times w \times c}\) that contains both real \(R(\mathbf{x _i)\) and imaginary part \(I(\mathbf{x _i)\).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-314: Next, we compute the amplitude spectrum \(A(\mathbf{x _i)(u, v)\) using $A(x)(u, v) = \sqrt{R^2(x)(u, v) + I^2(x)(u, v)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-315: It reveals the intensity of the frequency content, e.g., high-frequency amplitudes highlight edges and fine details while low-frequency amplitudes emphasize the overall structure and gradual changes.
elements.sent-2411-316: Then, we filter out low-frequency elements using mask $M(u, v) = \mathbbm{1}\left( \left(u < \frac{h {4 \lor u > \frac{3h {4}\right) \lor \left(v < \frac{w {4 \lor v > \frac{3w {4}\right) \right) $ to emphasize the high-frequency components $G(x)(u, v) $ that are more likely to indicate shifts in distribution: \begin{equation}G(x)(u, v) = A(x)(u, v) \cdot M(u, v). \label{hf1}\end{equation}.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-317: The process is formalized as: \begin{equation}\min_{\mathbf{C}, \mathbf{Z}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\| \mathbf{A}_{hf,i} - \mathbf{C}_{\mathbf{Z}_i} \right\|^2_2, \label{kmeans}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{A _{hf,i = \text{vec (G(x_i))$, $\mathbf{C \in \mathbb{C ^{K \times d}$, $\mathbf{Z \in \{1, \dots, K\}^n$ denotes the 1D high-frequency component of the amplitude spectrum, the centroids of the clusters and the cluster assignments for each image.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-318: Test-time fine-tuning is then decentralized across these clusters, allowing for specialized adaptation within each subgroup: For each cluster \( k \), we adapt a specialized model \( q_{\theta_k (y|x) \) that is fine-tuned using only the data within that cluster: \begin{equation}\theta_k^* = \arg\min_{\theta_k} \mathbb{E}_{x \sim {p}_{t,k}} \left[ \mathcal{L}(q_{\theta_k}(x)) \right], \label{update}\end{equation} where \( {p _{t,k \) represents the data distribution within cluster \( k \), and \( \mathcal{L \) is the loss function.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-319: The predictions of each iteration are collected and sorted after the local fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-320: To integrate knowledge from all subnetworks and prevent degradation on specific subdomains, we periodically aggregation their parameters at intervals of time $T$: \begin{equation}\mathbf{\theta}_{\text{base}} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \left( \frac{|\mathcal{D}_k|}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} |\mathcal{D}_j|} \mathbf{\theta}_k \right), \label{comm}\end{equation} where \( |\mathcal{D _k| \) denotes sample number in cluster \( k \).}
elements.sent-2411-321: The updated parameters \( \mathbf{\theta _{\text{base \) are then distributed back to each subnetwork, initializing them for the next batch of training: $\mathbf{\theta _k \leftarrow \mathbf{\theta _{\text{base }$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-322: To improve target data quality, TTA methods commonly adopt data augmentation as a practical strategy to enhance model generalization.
elements.sent-2411-323: However, conventional augmentation techniques -- typically borrowed from standard computer vision tasks such as rotation, cropping, and mixup -- are primarily tailored for single-shift scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-324: They often fail to provide the targeted, distribution shifts-aware augmentation as required under mixed domain shifts.
elements.sent-2411-325: To this end, we propose a frequency-based augmentation strategy tailored for TTA in mixed-shift environments.
elements.sent-2411-326: Specifically, our method perturbs the amplitude components of target samples in the Fourier space, enabling targeted augmentation with respect to each sample's underlying distribution.
elements.sent-2411-327: By manipulating the frequency-domain characteristics, our approach generates diverse yet distribution-consistent variations, thereby enriching subdomain representations and facilitating robust model adaptation.
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Framework of Frequency-based Decentralized Learning and Augmentation}\label{algo:FreDA} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require Step $t$, Input batch $\mathbf{X}= \{x_1, x_2,..., x_n\} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times h \times w \times c}$, Pretrained source model $q_\theta$, Initialize Feature Repository and Local Sample Pool $\mathcal{R}, \mathcal{S}_k \leftarrow \emptyset$, CLUSTER\_NUM $K$, KMEANS\_SIZE $N$, COMM\_INTERVAL $f$; \Statex \textbf{\textcolor{black!40}{Step 1: Extract Frequency Features}} \For{$i = 1$ to $n$} \State $\mathbf{A}_{hf,i} \leftarrow \text{vec}(\text{G}(\mathbf{x}_i))$ \Comment{Extract high-freq components} \EndFor \Statex \textbf{\textcolor{black!40}{Step 2: Dynamic Clustering}} \State $\mathcal{R} \leftarrow \mathcal{R} \cup \{\mathbf{A}_{hf,i}\}_{i=1}^n$ \Comment{Frequency Information Repository} \State $\mathcal{R} \leftarrow \mathcal{R}[(|\mathcal{R}| - N + 1) : ]$ \Comment{Keep the last $N$ entries for kmeans clustering} \State $(\mathbf{C}_t, \mathbf{Z}) \leftarrow \text{K-means}(\mathcal{R}, K, \mathbf{C}_{t-1})$ \Comment{\textit{Obtain Cluster Labels $\mathbf{Z} = \{Z_i\}_{i=1}^{n}$ (Eq.~\ref{kmeans})}} \Statex \textbf{\textcolor{black!40}{Step 3: Local Model Training}} \For {cluster $k \in \{1, \ldots, K\}$} \State $\mathcal{S}_k \leftarrow \mathcal{S}_k \cup \{x_i \mid Z_i = k\}$ \Comment{Gather samples for cluster $k$} \State $\mathcal{S}_k \leftarrow \mathcal{S}_k[(|\mathcal{S}_k| - n + 1) : ]$ \Comment{Keep the last $batch\_size = n$ entries} \State $\mathcal{S}_k' \leftarrow \text{select\_samples}(\mathcal{S}_k)$ \Comment{\textit{Select samples (Eq.~\ref{select})}} \For{each $x_i \in \mathcal{S}_k'$} \State $\tilde{x}_i \leftarrow \text{augment}(x_i)$ \Comment{\textit{Augment data (Eq.~\ref{generation})}} \State $\text{Train}(q_{\theta_k},x_i, \tilde{x}_i)$ \Comment{\textit{Train local model (Eq.~\ref{update})}} \EndFor \EndFor \Statex \textbf{\textcolor{black!40}{Step 4: Compile Predictions}} \State $\mathbf{Y} \leftarrow \text{collect\_sort}(\{q_{\theta_k}(\mathbf{X})\})$ \Comment{Collect and sort predictions} \Statex \textbf{\textcolor{black!40}{Step 5: Base Model Enhancement}} \State If t \% $f$ == 0 : \Comment{\textit{Model Communication with interval $f$ (Eq.\ref{comm})}} \State \quad $\mathbf{\theta}_{\text{global}} \leftarrow \sum_{k=1}^K w_k \theta_k$ \State \quad$\mathbf{\theta}_k \leftarrow \mathbf{\theta}_{\text{global}}$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-329: This ensures that selected samples exhibit significant deviations from previous predictions in class distribution and lower prediction uncertainty.

elements.sent-2411-400: Recent advances highlight frequency-based techniques as powerful tools for domain transfer.
elements.sent-2411-401: In domain generalization, frequency analysis has revealed critical insights into model robustness and learning dynamics~\cite{freq1,freq2,freq3,freq4,freq5,vaish2024fourier,freq6,freq7 .}
elements.sent-2411-402: For domain adaptation, interpolating image amplitude spectra across styles has proven effective in reducing domain gaps and preventing overfitting to low-level statistics~\cite{fda1,fda2,fda3,fda4 .}
elements.sent-2411-403: Motivated by these advancements, we discover that frequency information inherently captures domain characteristics, making it a valuable medium for decoupling mixed target domains -- an aspect largely unexplored in prior work.
elements.sent-2411-404: On this basis, we propose FreDA, the first framework addressing TTA under mixed domain shifts in Fourier space, with a decentralized adaptation and perturbation mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-405: TTA under mixed distribution shifts shares similarities with multi-target unsupervised domain adaptation (MT-UDA)~\cite{mt1,mt2,ocda1,ocda2 , yet diverges critically in complexity: while MT-UDA assumes static, predefined target domains and leverages labeled source data for explicit domain alignment, TTA operates with no access to source data and must adapt to dynamic, unpredicTABLE target streams in an online manner.}
elements.sent-2411-406: This eliminates direct source-target discrepancy computation and demand robust incremental adaptation rather than offline multi-domain optimization.
elements.sent-2411-407: These differences highlight the need for novel methodologies tailored specifically to TTA, going beyond the solutions developed for MT-UDA.
elements.subsection-2411-021: Relation to Decentralized, Federated, Distributed Learning
elements.sent-2411-408: This work also intersects with decentralized, federated, and distributed learning due to splitting data batches into disjoint subsets and applying decentralized model adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-409: First, while decentralized learning focuses on non-i.i.d.
elements.sent-2411-410: By proactively splitting this data into disjoint subsets, we expose latent non-i.i.d.
elements.sent-2411-411: Second, federated learning considers data privacy and model collaborations within decentralized learning~\cite{fedavg .}
elements.sent-2411-412: In our case, as target samples are mixed in a batch, data privacy is not a concern.
elements.sent-2411-413: However, similar to federated learning, our approach also involves weight aggregation over subnetworks to enhance their base models before next batch adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-414: Third, distributed learning aims to improve training efficiency on large-scale datasets by partitioning data for synchronized training~\cite{mcdonald2010distributed .}
elements.sent-2411-415: In contrast, our method operates in a real-time fine-tuning context with limited data at one time, hence scalability is less of a concern.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
elements.sent-2411-416: In this paper, we study TTA under mixed distribution shifts and move beyond conventional whole-batch homogeneous adaptation paradigms.
elements.sent-2411-417: By revisiting distribution shifts from a frequency-domain perspective, we observe that distribution heterogeneity often manifests in Fourier space -- for instance, high-frequency components tend to carry domain-specific variations.
elements.sent-2411-418: This motivates us to un-mix heterogeneous data streams using high-frequency texture cues, making diverse shift patterns more tractable.
elements.sent-2411-419: Despite their success, current TTA models are often limited to ideal testing conditions, typically involving homogeneous test samples with similar types of distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-420: In reality, distribution shifts are mixed, overlapping, and even conflicting~\cite{mt1,mt2,yao2025scmix .}
elements.sent-2411-421: While recent efforts have extended TTA to continually changing environments~\cite{wang2022continual , they typically assume a uniform target domain at each time step, as illustrated in the continual setting in Fig.~\ref{fig:tta_problem ~(a).}}
elements.sent-2411-422: Although these strategies aim to improve robustness under dynamic conditions, they lack the capacity to disentangle or localize domain variations within a batch.
elements.sent-2411-423: As a result, they often misalign conflicting domain shifts, failing to be deployed in realistic, heterogeneous data streams (Fig.~\ref{fig:tta_problem ~(b)).}
elements.figure-2411-026: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{Figures/tta_problem.pdf} \caption{(a) Illustration of three TTA scenarios with single, continual, and mixed domain shifts. (b) Classification error rate on CIFAR-10-C across different TTA settings: performance of conventional models drops sharply under mixed domain shifts. } \label{fig:tta_problem}
elements.sent-2411-424: To tackle this, we propose shifting from coarse, homogeneous adaptation to a fine-grained strategy that explicitly disentangles heterogeneous shifts.
elements.sent-2411-425: These models adapt independently to their respective data streams while periodically synchronize through parameter exchange.
elements.sent-2411-426: The aggregated parameters form a shared base model, which is then used to update the local models, ensuring coordinated adaptation across domains.
elements.figure-2411-027: \caption{Comparison of different transfer learning settings.} \label{tab:da_comparison} \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2} \setlength\tabcolsep{7pt} 0.9\textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{lcccc} Topic No Source Data$^*$ No Target Labels$^*$ Online Adaptation Model Agnostic {Supervised Domain Adaptation} $\times$ $\times$ $\times$ -- {Unsupervised Domain Adaptation} $\times$ $\checkmark$ $\times$ -- {Source-free Domain Adaptation} $\checkmark$ $\checkmark$ $\times$ -- {Online Test-time Training} $\checkmark$ $\checkmark$ $\checkmark$ $\times$ \rowcolor{gray!10} {Online Test-time Adaptation} $\checkmark$ $\checkmark$ $\checkmark$ $\checkmark$ \end{tabular} } \caption*{ Note: $^*$ denotes particular constraints during the adaptation process.}
elements.sent-2411-427: This issue has been extensively studied under the umbrella of transfer learning~\cite{pan2009survey,zhang2022transfer , which aims to transfer knowledge across domains.}
elements.sent-2411-428: Depending on the data availability, previous DA can be roughly classified into: Supervised DA using labeled data in both source and target domains during training; Unsupervised DA, which relies on labeled source data and unlabeled target data; and Source-free DA, that adapts to the target domain without access to source data, typically due to privacy or transmission constraints.
elements.sent-2411-429: While traditional DA methods assume access to the entire target distribution before adaptation, real-world deployment often involves receiving data as an online stream, where samples arrive sequentially and the full distribution is never observed.
elements.sent-2411-430: This streaming nature of the target domain breaks the assumption of global access, making conventional domain adaptation infeasible.
elements.sent-2411-431: To handle this constraint, two prominent test-time adaptation paradigms have emerged.
elements.sent-2411-432: Online Test-time Training (OTTT)~\cite{TTT,liu2021ttt++,TTT_tkde introduces an auxiliary task -- often self-supervised -- during pre-training.}
elements.sent-2411-433: At test time, the model optimizes this auxiliary objective to adapt to the target distribution accordingly; Online Test-time Adaptation (OTTA)~\cite{wang2021tent,niu2022towards,su2024unraveling poses a more demanding and practical scenario, where the model must adapt on-the-fly to the test stream without any prior modification during training.}
elements.sent-2411-434: Our work falls under the OTTA setting, where we propose a generalizable and robust adaptation framework that maintains strong performance across diverse distribution shifts and corruptions -- without relying on source data or altering the pre-training process.
elements.figure-2411-028: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth,clip, trim=14.3cm 4.5cm 18cm 2cm]{Figures/high_freq_sep.pdf} \caption{ (a) Visual heterogeneity across five healthcare centers in Camelyon17 dataset: Example patches showcase diverse domain shifts that characterize mixed target domains. (b) Latent features of samples from pretrained models fail to separate different target subdomains, showing significant overlap. (c) High-frequency information enable distinct separation of target subdomains, contrasting with the significant overlap in (b).} \label{fig:high_freq_effect}

elements.section-2411-018: Un-mixing TTA under Mixed Distribution Shifts: A Fourier Perspective
elements.sent-2411-441: While Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) methods perform well under single-type shifts, they degrade under mixed distribution shifts that are ubiquitous in real-world deployment.
elements.sent-2411-442: To address this, we advocate a paradigm of proactive distribution management: before any parameter update, we resolve heterogeneity by decomposing the input stream in frequency space.
elements.sent-2411-443: Our key insight is that different distributions leave distinct spectral footprints, which can be utilized to disentangle the hetergenous data stream.
elements.sent-2411-444: As shown in Fig.\ref{fig:high_freq_effect (b-c), high-frequency components naturally separate different domain shifts, in contrast to the substantial overlap observed in the sample features from a pre-trained model.}
elements.sent-2411-445: This proactive step transforms an ill-posed, entangled adaptation problem into a set of well-conditioned homogeneous subproblems.
elements.sent-2411-447: In its original form, TBN computes statistical information from the entire target batch and directly replaces the source parameters for online adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-448: While effective under single-domain shifts, this whole-batch strategy produces biased statistics under mixed-domain shifts, leading to suboptimal adaptation (Fig.~\ref{fig:comparedClassVisual (a)).}
elements.sent-2411-449: In contrast, our decentralized approach first applies high-frequency clustering to partition the batch into locally homogeneous clusters.
elements.sent-2411-450: BN statistics are then computed within each cluster, enabling localized adaptation that can effectively prevent cross-domain contamination.
elements.sent-2411-451: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:comparedClassVisual (b), this decentralized strategy yields tighter within-class cohesion and sharper between-class boundaries than centralized adaptation.}
elements.sent-2411-452: As outlined in the previous section, distinguishing samples from different distribution shifts provides a promising direction for TTA.
elements.sent-2411-453: Building on this insight, we introduce Frequency-based Decentralized Adaptation (FreDA), which partitions target samples in the Fourier space into homogeneous subdomains, enabling more accurate adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-454: We further enhance this process with a augmentation strategy that diversifies each subdomain in the Fourier space to improve model robustness.
elements.sent-2411-455: We first introduce Frequency-based Decentralized Learning framework.
elements.sent-2411-456: It begins by extracting high-frequency information from the pixel space to partition target samples into homogeneous subsets, disclosing the latent target subdomains contained within a batch.
elements.sent-2411-457: We then perform a decentralized adaptation, where multiple local models -- sharing the same architecture -- independently adapt to their assigned subsets.
elements.sent-2411-458: At regular intervals, the parameters of these local models are aggregated to form a stronger base model, which is subsequently used to update the local models for the next adaptation phase.
elements.sent-2411-459: Detailed procedures are provided in the following subsections.
elements.sent-2411-460: To address this, we introduce a frequency-based augmentation strategy tailored for our decentralized paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-461: The method perturbs the amplitude components of target samples in the Fourier space, generating variations that are diverse yet consistent with each sample’s underlying distribution.
elements.sent-2411-462: This targeted augmentation enriches subdomain representations and strengthens model robustness across heterogeneous shifts.
elements.sent-2411-463: To facilitate theoretical understanding of our method, we adopt the expansion-based framework~\cite{wei2020theoretical , which offers a principled analysis of pseudo-labeling under local perturbations and neighborhood consistency, and connects these properties directly to upper bounds on model error rates.}
elements.sent-2411-464: We first revisit the key definitions and theorems, then demonstrate how our method -- via two core mechanisms, Frequency-coherent Partitioning and Augmentation-induced Expansion -- effectively tightens this generalization bound, underscoring its theoretical strength in the presence of mixed domain shifts.

elements.sent-2411-486: TTA under mixed distribution shifts shares similarities with multi-target unsupervised domain adaptation (MT-UDA)~\cite{mt1,mt2,ocda1,ocda2 , yet diverges critically in complexity: while MT-UDA assumes static, predefined target domains and leverages labeled source data for explicit domain alignment, TTA operates with no access to source data and must adapt to dynamic, unpredictable target streams in an online manner.}

elements.sent-2411-487: This paper advances Test-Time Adaptation by tackling the practical challenges posed by heterogeneous data streams.
elements.sent-2411-488: We introduce FreDA, a frequency-based decentralized adaptation framework that partitions incoming samples and performs localized adaptation, while employing {distribution-aware augmentation to effectively handle mixed distribution shifts.}
elements.sent-2411-489: Empirical results across multiple benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, highlighting its potential to drive future research on adapting to dynamic and diverse domain shifts.
elements.sent-2411-490: Future Work: FreDA provides a principled framework for adapting to mixed distribution shifts, and we believe several promising directions could further enhance its capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-491: Second, our frequency-based augmentation is currently applied at the sample level, which is well-suited for classification.
elements.sent-2411-492: Extending it to patch-level operations in spatial–frequency space may enrich the model’s granularity and open new possibilities for fine-grained tasks such as segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-493: Together, these directions offer natural extensions to broaden FreDA’s applicability in increasingly realistic and dynamic environments.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
